title: From autopsy to PET to cerebrospinal fluid to blood: Quantifying reference-standard dependence in Alzheimer's disease amyloid biomarker validation
abstract: Background Blood-based biomarkers are increasingly used as scalable front-line tools for Alzheimer's disease amyloid evaluation, but most are validated against amyloid positron emission tomography (PET) and/or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) rather than neuropathology. In a chained validation cascade (neuropathology‚ÜíPET‚ÜíCSF‚Üíblood), reported performance may depend strongly on the comparator and on how ‚Äúgray-zone‚Äù (indeterminate) results are handled. Objective To quantify benchmark dependence and gray-zone policy sensitivity in amyloid biomarker validation and characterize plasma-to-neuropathology performance inference from imperfect proxies. Methods We assembled a fully auditable evidence core spanning the amyloid cascade with reconstructable 2‚Äâ√ó‚Äâ2 tables. We recomputed positive percent agreement (PPA) and negative percent agreement (NPA) with 95% Wilson confidence intervals under a prespecified primary policy (exclude indeterminates) and two sensitivity policies (indeterminate‚Üínegative; indeterminate‚Üípositive). Reference-swap drift across benchmarks was summarized with Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals. For plasma-to-neuropathology inference, we combined plasma-PET agreement with tracer-specific PET-autopsy anchors and reported (i) chained-proxy point implications under conditional independence and (ii) partial-identification bounds without conditional independence across prevalence scenarios (œÄ‚Äâ=‚Äâ10‚Äì50%). Results Under the primary policy, Lumipulse plasma pTau217/AŒ≤42 showed similar agreement across PET, CSF, and composite comparators (PPA 0.97‚Äì0.98; NPA ‚âà0.91), with drift intervals spanning zero. Indeterminates were frequent (‚àº19‚Äì20%) and dominated apparent shifts: indeterminate‚Üínegative reduced PPA by 0.13‚Äì0.21, whereas indeterminate‚Üípositive reduced NPA by 0.17‚Äì0.21. Chained-proxy implications for plasma versus neuropathology varied by PET tracer anchor (sensitivity 0.86‚Äì0.96; specificity 0.81‚Äì0.91), while bounds were wider (sensitivity 0.83‚Äì1.00; specificity 0.81‚Äì0.98). Conclusions Gray-zone policy is a first-order driver of reported blood-test performance, and proxy-to-proxy agreement does not uniquely identify plasma-to-neuropathology accuracy without explicit assumptions.
